{"decision":"warn","decision_source":"pre_claim_intake","detail":"system_unready: This child claims to own 'part 3 of 3' of parent ddx-6a2ce3bd's scope, but its body reproduces the parent's full PROBLEM/ROOT CAUSE/PROPOSED FIX/IN-SCOPE FILES verbatim (provider_spawn.go:91, runner_test.go:29/56, work_resource_exhausted_test.go:80, try_test.go:823, doctor_test.go:85, fixture_repo.go:116) without identifying which of those producers is this child's distinct slice versus parts 1 and 2. No sibling bead IDs are referenced, so an executing agent cannot tell what to implement without either redoing the whole parent fix (risking duplicate/conflicting work with siblings) or guessing a partition. The AC also does not disambiguate: it runs the full ./internal/config/... ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... surface with no Test* name or -run filter scoped to a specific producer, so it cannot itself signal which subset is 'part 3'. IN-SCOPE FILES additionally hedges the helper location ('config/execution_worktree_root.go or a new scratch-temp helper package'), leaving unresolved whether this child or a sibling owns creating the shared helper. The five prior_attempts are all generic 'session closed before Stop hook' infra failures with no bead-content blocker signal, so they don't independently drive this classification, but the repeated pattern is worth flagging to the operator alongside the scope issue. A narrow semantics-preserving rewrite is not safe here because determining the correct file partition requires knowledge of sibling children's actual scope, which isn't available in this bead's fields.","fingerprint":"4d5d391d5badba97844c7bd32530d19a59c2d71931ae86c512198b029ad132f6","policy_mode":"warn-only","reason":"system_unready","rule_id":"pre_claim_intake.system_unready","suggested_action":"revise the rewrite so it preserves every explicit commitment","warning_fingerprint":"4d5d391d5badba97844c7bd32530d19a59c2d71931ae86c512198b029ad132f6"}
